hard.next_step: 5.8.26 KC: Secure NDA Establish: an agenda and biz plan Deeper Discovery set for week of 5.25
hard.ais_why:
* The buyer showed real interest in the AI concept: he "Expressed enthusiasm for the concept" of a Teams-based conversational/AI-guided procurement assistant and autonomous bidding, and asked for a use-case-driven demo/trial.
* Enthusiasm is heavily gated by feasibility: "uncertain about the costs and ease of implementation," a legacy IBM AS/400 ERP with a "failed SAP S/4HANA" attempt, and "95% of processing facility staff lack digital proficiency."
* Early and ROI-focused: discussion centered on where AI could have realistic short-term impact (fragmented janitorial spend); next steps are a scoped demo and a sourcing ROI analysis - no POC or budget yet. Curious, not Hungry.

north_star_verdict.headline: Two genuinely engaged discovery calls (8 May, 5 Jun) proved David Brothers loves the agentic/Teams vision, but he is a passive IC-level contact with no budget access, the buyer has gone silent since 5 Jun with two rep follow-ups unanswered, and the deeper discovery planned for the week of 25 May never happened.
north_star_verdict.math: Close 13 Nov 2026 (~130 days out). Created 8 May 2026, ~59 days old, still in Qualified. Time in Qualified since qualification date 12 May: ~55 days. Last buyer two-way exchange was the 5 Jun demo call (~31 days ago); the only touches since are rep-initiated Clari emails on 5 Jun and 17 Jun with no buyer reply. The planned deeper-discovery session for the week of 25 May is ~6 weeks overdue with no logged occurrence. Enterprise motion is 12-15 months; this deal has not completed discovery, has no EB, no champion, no scoped use-case demo, no pricing agreed, so a credible close is well beyond 13 Nov. Pace required to hold any date: re-open David, complete deeper discovery, and run a scoped ROI proof within 30 days.
evidence: Deal created 8 May 2026, still in Qualified after ~59 days; forecast already Pipeline, which is the honest category for a pre-champion, pre-EB discovery deal.

David Brothers (Decision Maker contact role) is genuinely sold on the concept: 'I see a lot of benefit... I'm sold on the why' and 'this sounds really cool, it would really make a lot of the stuff that we do easier' (Avoma 5 Jun).

But David is a passive 'long term prospect': 'the lift of getting from this conversation to actually starting a contract... I'm going to have to contemplate how to take that from this conversation to something actionable' (Avoma 5 Jun).
No economic buyer engaged: David sits in finance and only references a VP of Finance, a director of distribution and a VP of processing (Avoma 8 May), none of whom has joined a call on a $110k ask.

Implementation fear is David's stated #1 roadblock: 'we're still operating an AS/400... we had a failed SAP S/4HANA attempt two years ago... the implementation is gonna be very difficult, the number one roadblock' (Avoma 8 May).
Buyer has gone quiet: the deeper-discovery session set for the week of 25 May never happened, and rep follow-ups on 5 Jun and 17 Jun drew no reply.

Pricing is not taboo: Rick gave a $75k-$125k annual ballpark on 8 May and David engaged on user counts, but David still asked on 5 Jun for cost documentation to know whether 'this is a $2M annual project or a $50k annual' conversation.

metrics.narrative: The pain is real and articulated but never quantified. David Brothers walked Rick through his live use case on 8 May: janitorial/sanitation ('jansan') supplies bought across many sites where even the ~5 locations buying as one unit are 'completely fragmented', so 'tracking down the invoices and what we've been purchasing for the last year for toilet paper, paper towels from each location, then determining the price per square foot... gathering all that data is a real pain in the ***.' He confirmed the value on 5 Jun: 'it would take a lot of stuff off our plate that we don't need.' But no number was ever put to it: no janitorial spend baseline, no FTE hours lost, no cycle-time or error-rate benchmark, and no hard-savings target. Rick offered the lever on 5 Jun, a sourcing ROI / value-engineering exercise ('our value engineering group will ask you about 50 questions and calculate your potential value... based on our customers'), but David has not scheduled it. Quantifying this via the ROI workshop is the ranked move.
sources: Avoma 8 May discovery call, David Brothers (janitorial/jansan fragmentation)
Avoma 5 Jun demo call, David Brothers
Avoma 5 Jun, Rick Taranek offered value-engineering / sourcing ROI

economic_buyer.narrative: No economic buyer is identified or engaged. David Brothers, the sole Salesforce contact (Internal spend Category Coordinator, Decision Maker role), sits inside the finance department and himself has no budget mandate for a company-wide purchase. He described the real approval structure on 8 May: he sees the tool 'helping my team... and interact with our VP of finance... as we work with the director of distribution or the vice president of processing, the people that are always kind of making these executive oversight decisions.' None of those roles has been named to us or joined a call. David also signalled his own budget uncertainty on 5 Jun: he needs to know whether this is 'a $2M annual project or a $50k annual' conversation before he can position it internally. Because the budget owner is only referenced by role, not named or met, the EB is a genuine gap; the move is to have David introduce his VP of Finance into the ROI workshop so the budget owner is mapped and in the room.
sources: Salesforce OpportunityContactRole: David Brothers, Decision Maker (sole contact)
Avoma 8 May, David Brothers (VP of Finance / director of distribution / VP of processing referenced)
Avoma 5 Jun, David Brothers (unsure of budget scale)

decision_criteria.narrative: David's evaluation lens is clear even though no formal criteria doc exists. His decisive test is not product capability but implementation feasibility in a low-tech environment: on 8 May he said 'at this point it's not so much about educating myself on why this would make sense... it's more like realistically bringing something like this on within our company and how that would actually look,' and 'implementation within Pacific Seafood is extremely difficult for enterprise level technology.' His secondary criteria are short-term, realistic ROI on fragmented indirect spend (the janitorial use case), simple adoption for 'technologically unsophisticated' users (he twice asked to see the Microsoft Teams experience and reacted well: 'I like it, that sounds really useful'), and cost transparency. These are confirmed from the calls; there is no RFP, scorecard, or written requirement yet, so the criteria remain inferred-from-conversation rather than documented.
sources: Avoma 8 May discovery call, David Brothers (implementation feasibility as the decisive test)
Avoma 5 Jun demo call, David Brothers (Teams simplicity, scoped use case)

decision_process.narrative: There is no defined decision process yet; the deal is running as exploratory education. The call sequence shows two vendor-led sessions (8 May overview with Kevin Cipollaro, 5 Jun product overview after Rick Taranek took over ownership) attended only by David and, on 5 Jun, Oren. No buying committee, no timeline, no evaluation stages and no approval chain have been established. David explicitly deferred any process: 'I'm going to have to contemplate how to take this from this conversation to actually turning this into something actionable' and 'let me think about how serious this starts to look, and then I'll follow up' (5 Jun). The next-step field itself only reaches 'Secure NDA, establish an agenda and biz plan, deeper discovery week of 5.25' and that discovery never occurred. Building the process (who decides, by when, what gates) with David is a prerequisite before any milestone can be forecast.
sources: Avoma 8 May and 5 Jun calls (attendees David Brothers, Oren)
Salesforce Next_Step__c (NDA / agenda / deeper discovery, week of 25 May)
Avoma 5 Jun, David Brothers (no forward commitment)

paper_process.narrative: Contracting mechanics are untouched, which is normal at Qualified. The only paper signal is the Salesforce next step 'Secure NDA' set 8 May; there is no evidence the NDA was signed, and no MSA, order form, SOW, legal, procurement channel or InfoSec discussion has occurred on either call. David repeatedly framed a contract as distant: 'the lift of getting from this conversation to actually starting a contract with you guys... I'd say we're a long term prospect' (5 Jun). No contract-expiration or renewal forcing function exists, since this is a net-new build on an AS/400 shop with no incumbent procurement suite. Visibility here will only come once discovery and ROI create enough intent to justify an NDA and a scoped commercial; there is nothing to redline today.
sources: Salesforce Next_Step__c (Secure NDA, 8 May)
Avoma 5 Jun, David Brothers ('long term prospect')

identify_pain.narrative: The operational pain is confirmed and owned by David Brothers and his indirect-procurement team in finance. Primary pain: fragmented janitorial/jansan sourcing across many decentralized sites where he manually 'tracks down invoices... for the last year for toilet paper, paper towels from each location, then determines the price per square foot,' calling it 'a real pain in the ***' (8 May). Secondary pain: rogue/maverick and manual purchasing where employees 'call the purchasing group and the purchasing group makes the purchases for them' (5 Jun). Underneath sits an organizational pain David named as the biggest obstacle, not a benefit: extremely low digital maturity ('very technologically unsophisticated people... 95%... really not even doing emails'), a legacy AS/400 ERP and a failed SAP S/4HANA attempt two years ago. The buying pain is genuine; the risk is that the implementation pain outweighs it into a no-decision.
sources: Avoma 8 May discovery call, David Brothers (janitorial fragmentation, AS/400, failed SAP)
Avoma 5 Jun demo call, David Brothers (maverick/manual purchasing)

champion.narrative: No champion is secured. David Brothers is engaged and understands the value but is passive and has taken no internal action to advance the deal. He is the sole buyer-side contact, has not introduced a single colleague across two calls, and framed himself as an explorer rather than an advocate: 'this is definitely a long term conversation... there's a chance we could turn into something that's really cool, there's a chance it doesn't... it's hard to say at this point' and 'let me think about how serious this starts to look, and then I'll follow up' (5 Jun). What has developed: genuine product affinity and a clear personal pain (janitorial sourcing). What is still missing: any evidence of internal selling, access to the VP of Finance / processing / distribution leaders he names, and a willingness to drive a next step. Champion strength: weak. Turning David from interested contact into an active champion, by giving him a quantified ROI story he can carry to his VP of Finance, ranks ahead of any commercial step.
sources: Salesforce OpportunityContactRole: David Brothers, Decision Maker (sole contact)
Avoma 5 Jun, David Brothers (passive, non-committal)
Avoma 8 May and 5 Jun (no internal introductions made)

competition.narrative: This is not a vendor bake-off; the real contest is Zycus versus doing nothing. David mentioned one alternative on 8 May, Zip ('I think I also talked to a company called Zip'), but immediately de-emphasized it ('I'm not comparing you guys to Zip, really'), and Zip only overlaps our iSource module while we pitch end-to-end source-to-pay, so it is a low, dormant threat. The dominant competitor is inertia driven by implementation fear: David's low digital maturity, AS/400 legacy and failed SAP history make a no-decision the base case, and he said so plainly ('there's a chance it doesn't'). Our confirmed wedge is the Microsoft Teams experience (David asked to see it twice and reacted well) plus a phased, single-use-case start on his janitorial spend rather than an enterprise big-bang. We win by proving fast, low-risk time-to-value on that one use case and quantifying it, not by out-featuring a rival.
sources: Avoma 8 May, David Brothers (Zip mentioned then de-emphasized)
Avoma 5 Jun, David Brothers (implementation fear / do-nothing)
Avoma 8 May and 5 Jun (Teams integration wedge)

deal_movement.summary: The deal peaked at the 5 Jun demo and has cooled since. Ownership moved from Kevin Cipollaro to Rick Taranek on 3 Jun and the division was reclassified to New Markets on 3 Jul, but on the buyer side there has been no two-way engagement since the 5 Jun call; the deeper-discovery session planned for the week of 25 May never happened, and rep-initiated Clari follow-ups on 5 Jun and 17 Jun went unanswered. Ball is with the deal team to re-open David directly.

items.change: Product overview demo delivered; David reaffirmed interest but stayed non-committal ('I'm sold on the why... there's a chance it doesn't')
Ownership transferred from Kevin Cipollaro to Rick Taranek
Rep-initiated Clari follow-up emails to David and Oren; no buyer reply logged
Planned deeper-discovery session (week of 25 May) not held

competitive_position.summary: The single strongest current threat is do-nothing inertia, not a rival vendor. David's low digital maturity, AS/400 legacy and failed SAP S/4HANA history make a no-decision the base case (8 May, still the live risk on 5 Jun). Zip is the only named alternative but David de-emphasized it and it overlaps only our iSource module, so it is a low, dormant threat. There is no RFP, shortlist or active bake-off. How we win overall: prove fast, low-risk time-to-value on David's fragmented janitorial-spend use case, quantify it with the sourcing ROI workshop, and use the Microsoft Teams adoption wedge to defuse the implementation fear.

competitors.quote: there's a chance that we could turn into something that's really cool, there's a chance it doesn't... it's hard to say at this point... I'm going to have to contemplate how to take this from this conversation to actually turning this into something actionable
competitors.source: Avoma 5 Jun demo call, David Brothers
competitors.how_we_win: Beat inertia with a scoped single-use-case pilot on David's janitorial spend (not enterprise big-bang), a quantified ROI proof, and a Teams-first, phased rollout narrative that directly answers the AS/400 / failed-SAP implementation fear.

competitors.name: Zip
competitors.quote: I think I also talked to a company called Zip... I'm not comparing you guys to Zip, really
competitors.source: Avoma 8 May discovery call, David Brothers
competitors.how_we_win: Zip overlaps only our iSource module; position the end-to-end single-code-base platform (sourcing, contracts, P2P, spend analysis) and agentic Merlin negotiation as the differentiation David himself acknowledged ('Zip is narrower').

customer_expectations_fit.summary: David's expectations are dominated by feasibility, not features. He is aligned on the AI/Teams simplicity and the janitorial use-case value, but exposed on implementation realism in a low-digital environment and on cost transparency.

best_practice_check.summary: Early Qualified deal whose fate turns on beating do-nothing inertia and reaching budget authority, not on out-competing a rival.
flags: Multi-thread beyond David to his named VP of Finance to map budget authority before any pricing conversation lands.
Convert the janitorial-spend use case into a scoped, quantified ROI proof to overcome do-nothing inertia.
Answer the AS/400 / failed-SAP implementation fear head-on with a phased, Teams-first low-risk rollout story.

champion_strength.summary: No champion is secured. David Brothers understands and likes the solution but is passive, single-threaded, and has taken no internal action; he framed himself as a 'long term prospect' rather than an advocate. What has developed: genuine product affinity and a clear personal pain. What is missing: internal selling, EB access, and any drive on a next step.

ai_positioning_strength.summary: The agentic/Teams AI narrative landed well emotionally: David called autonomous negotiation and Teams-based intake 'really cool' and 'really useful'. But excitement is gated by feasibility, so the AI story is strong while the realism story is weak; the fix is proving the AI value on one concrete use case, not more capability breadth.

ai_fit_signal.summary: AIS 5.0 / Moderate is accurate: David expressed real enthusiasm for the Teams-based conversational assistant and autonomous bidding but is uncertain about cost and ease of implementation given AS/400 legacy and low digital proficiency. Curious, not hungry; the calls neither over- nor under-rate the field.

13 Nov close is a placeholder; deeper discovery overdue since the week of 25 May and buyer quiet since 5 Jun.

deal_scores_evidence.summary: Two genuinely engaged discovery calls proved David Brothers loves the agentic/Teams vision, but he is a passive IC-level contact with no budget authority, the buyer has gone silent since 5 Jun, and the real opponent is Pacific Seafood's do-nothing inertia and AS/400 implementation fear, not a rival vendor.

win_position.text: This is a very early Qualified deal riding on one passive contact: David Brothers (indirect spend coordinator) is sold on the vision ('I see a lot of benefit... I'm sold on the why', Avoma 5 Jun) but openly a 'long term prospect' with no path to budget yet.

No economic buyer is mapped or engaged: David sits in finance and only references a VP of Finance and processing/distribution execs (Avoma 8 May) who have never joined a call, so nobody with budget authority is in the room on the $110k ask.

The strongest opponent is do-nothing inertia: David's #1 stated roadblock is implementation in a low-digital, AS/400 shop after a failed SAP S/4HANA attempt ('the number one roadblock', Avoma 8 May), and he has gone quiet since 5 Jun with two rep follow-ups unanswered.

deal_momentum.text: Momentum has stalled: the deeper-discovery session planned for the week of 25 May never happened and no buyer reply has landed to the 5 Jun and 17 Jun follow-ups.
Commercial topics did enter early: Rick gave a $75k-$125k annual ballpark on 8 May and David engaged on user counts, so pricing is not taboo.

customer_commitment.text: David made no concrete commitment: he closed the 5 Jun demo with 'let me think about how serious this starts to look, and then I'll follow up' and has not reverted.
Engagement is single-threaded on one IC-level contact with no NDA signed and no ROI workshop scheduled.

deal_risk.text: Do-nothing is the live threat: low digital maturity ('very technologically unsophisticated people... 95%', Avoma 8 May) plus a failed SAP S/4HANA history make a no-decision the base case.
The 13 Nov close date is a placeholder: an early Qualified deal with no EB, no champion and no scoped demo cannot credibly close in roughly four months.

deal_momentum.evidence: Deeper discovery (week of 25 May) never held; buyer silent since 5 Jun; two rep follow-ups unanswered.

customer_commitment.evidence: David non-committal ('there's a chance it doesn't'); single-threaded; no NDA, no ROI workshop booked.

deal_risk.evidence: Do-nothing inertia driven by AS/400 legacy, failed SAP, and low digital maturity; no EB engaged.

scope_change.detail: Product scope unchanged vs the prior record; Rick positioned a phased start on intake + iSource as a sales approach, not a documented scope reduction.

customer_preference.evidence: David praised the product and asked deeper Zycus-specific questions (Teams demo twice, pricing ballpark) but gave no decisive commercial lean and stayed a 'long term prospect'.

business_case.evidence: Qualitative pain only (fragmented janitorial sourcing, manual data gathering 'a real pain'); no savings, FTE, or cycle-time numbers; ROI workshop offered but not scheduled.
